file_name: XACCT Technologies, Inc.SUPPORT AND MAINTENANCE AGREEMENT.txt

perturbation.original_text: Fees shall be payable within thirty (30) days of invoice which shall be exclusive of any applicable local, state, federal, use, excise, value added, GST or other taxes imposed on the fees payable for the Product Maintenance and Support Services which shall be the responsibility of Licensee.
perturbation.changed_text: Fees shall be payable as invoiced. All taxes imposed on the fees payable for the Product Maintenance and Support Services shall be the responsibility of Licensee.
perturbation.explanation: Removing the specific payment timeframe ('within thirty (30) days') introduces ambiguity and can be used to delay payments, which contradicts California's Prompt Payment laws regarding software licenses and related services.
perturbation.law_explanation: California Civil Code Section 3320 requires that software licensors adhere to strict payment deadlines. By removing the 30-day payment term, the contract is made non-compliant with the spirit and intent of the Prompt Payment Act as it pertains to software agreements, potentially harming the licensee by allowing the licensor to demand quicker and less reasonable payment terms.

GENERAL PROVISIONS [3274 - 9566] ( Heading of Division 4 amended by Stats. 1988, Ch. 160, Sec. 16. ) PART 1. RELIEF [3274 - 3428] ( Part 1 enacted 1872. ) TITLE 2. COMPENSATORY RELIEF [3281 - 3361] ( Title 2 enacted 1872. ) CHAPTER 2. Measure of Damages [[3300.] - 3361] ( Chapter 2 enacted 1872. ) ARTICLE 1. Damages for Breach of Contract [[3300.] - 3322] ( Article 1 enacted 1872. ) 3320. (a) In each contract for public works of improvement, entered into on or after January 1, 1996, the public agency shall pay to the prime design professional any progress payment within 30 days of receipt of a written demand for payment in accordance with the contract, and the final retention payment within 45 days of receipt of a written demand for payment in accordance with the contract. If the public agency disputes in good faith any portion of the amount due, it may withhold from the payment an amount not to exceed 150 percent of the disputed amount. The disputed amount withheld is not subject to any penalty authorized by this section. (b) If any amount is wrongfully withheld or is not timely paid in violation of this section, the prime design professional shall be entitled to a penalty of 1 1 / 2 percent for the improperly withheld amount, in lieu o
perturbation.justification: The original contract specifies a 30-day payment timeframe, while the changed version removes this timeframe, stating "Fees shall be payable as invoiced." This omission creates a contradiction because California Civil Code Section 3320, as indicated in Scraped Law Snippet 1, mandates that public agencies pay prime design professionals within 30 days of receiving a written demand for payment for public works contracts. Although the law snippet specifically mentions 'public works of improvement,' the Perturbation Explanation and Law Explanation suggest a broader application to software licenses and related services under California's Prompt Payment laws. By removing the 30-day term, the contract potentially violates the intent of prompt payment, particularly if interpreted to fall under similar legal principles.

perturbation.original_text: XACCT will use reasonable commercial efforts to provide maintenance releases and Minor Releases to the then-current embodiment of the Product that it provides to its customers generally. Maintenance Releases and Minor Releases may also include one copy of revisions to the documentation applicable to such maintenance releases and Minor Releases.
perturbation.changed_text: XACCT will provide maintenance releases and Minor Releases at its discretion. Documentation revisions for these releases are not guaranteed.
perturbation.explanation: The original text commits to 'reasonable commercial efforts' for maintenance releases. The modified text removes this commitment and introduces uncertainty about documentation updates, reducing the licensee's guaranteed support level and potentially violating implied warranty laws, especially the implied warranty of fitness for a particular purpose under California law.
perturbation.law_explanation: California Commercial Code Section 2315 outlines the implied warranty of fitness for a particular purpose. By removing the 'reasonable commercial efforts' clause and stating documentation revisions are not guaranteed, the modified text reduces the reliability of the product and documentation updates to the point of violating the implied warranty, particularly if the software requires specific updates to function properly in the licensee's environment.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: California Code, COM 2315. skip to content home accessibility FAQ feedback sitemap login x Quick Search: Bill Number Bill Keyword Home Bill Information California Law Publications Other Resources My Subscriptions My Favorites California Law >> Code Search >> Code Section Code Section Code: Select Code CONS BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Article: Section: Code: Select Code All BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Section: Keyword(s): Code Search Text Search Up^ << Previous Next >> cross-reference chaptered bills PDF | Add To My Favorites Search Phrase: Code Text Commercial Code - COM DIVISION 2. SALES [2101 - 2801] ( Division 2 enacted by Stats. 1963, Ch. 819. ) CHAPTER 3. General Obligation and Construction of Contract [2301 - 2328] ( Chapter 3 enacted by Stats. 1963, Ch. 819. ) 2315. Where the seller at the time of contracting has reason to know any particular purpose for which the goods are required and that the buyer is relying on the seller’s skill or judgment to select or furnish suitable goods, there is unless excluded or modified under the next section an implied warranty that the goods shall be fit for such purpose. (Enacted by Stats. 1963, Ch. 819.)
perturbation.justification: The modification removes the commitment to 'reasonable commercial efforts' for maintenance releases and introduces uncertainty about documentation updates. This weakens the implied warranty of fitness for a particular purpose. Scraped Law Snippet 1, referencing California Commercial Code Section 2315, states that 'Where the seller...has reason to know any particular purpose for which the goods are required and that the buyer is relying on the seller’s skill or judgment to select or furnish suitable goods, there is...an implied warranty that the goods shall be fit for such purpose.' The change reduces the reliability of the product's updates needed for it to remain fit for its purpose. Because the original text committed to 'reasonable commercial efforts' and the modified text removes that commitment, creating a contradiction, this fits the definition of Omission Legal Contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: All disputes arising under or relating to this Agreement shall be resolved exclusively in the appropriate state court in Santa Clara County, California or in the federal court in the Northern District of California, and it is explicitly agreed that no other court shall have such jurisdiction.
perturbation.changed_text: All disputes arising under or relating to this Agreement shall be resolved in a court of XACCT's choosing.
perturbation.explanation: The original clause specifies jurisdiction clearly. The modified clause is vague and allows XACCT to choose any court, potentially far from the licensee, violating the principle of fair venue and contradicting the consumer protection laws regarding venue selection, particularly where it creates undue hardship for the licensee.
perturbation.law_explanation: California Code of Civil Procedure Section 395 establishes rules for venue in California. The modified text, by allowing XACCT to choose any court, conflicts with these rules and could force the licensee to litigate in an inconvenient location, increasing their costs and hindering their ability to defend their rights, effectively violating principles of fair venue and access to justice.

OF CIVIL ACTIONS [307 - 1062.34] ( Part 2 enacted 1872. ) TITLE 4. OF THE PLACE OF TRIAL, RECLASSIFICATION, AND COORDINATION OF CIVIL ACTIONS [392 - 404.9] ( Heading of Title 4 amended by Stats. 1999, Ch. 344, Sec. 6. ) CHAPTER 1. Place of Trial [392 - 403] ( Heading of Chapter 1 amended by Stats. 1999, Ch. 344, Sec. 7. ) 395. (a) Except as otherwise provided by law and subject to the power of the court to transfer actions or proceedings as provided in this title, the superior court in the county where the defendants or some of them reside at the commencement of the action is the proper court for the trial of the action. If the action is for injury to person or personal property or for death from wrongful act or negligence, the superior court in either the county where the injury occurs or the injury causing death occurs or the county where the defendants, or some of them reside at the commencement of the action, is a proper court for the trial of the action. In a proceeding for dissolution of marriage, the superior court in the county where either the petitioner or respondent has been a resident for three months next preceding the commencement of the proceeding is the proper court for the trial of the proceeding. In a proceeding for nullit
perturbation.justification: The modified text, by allowing XACCT to choose any court, directly contradicts the principle established in California Code of Civil Procedure Section 395, as seen in Scraped Law Snippet 1, which designates the superior court in the county where the defendants reside as the proper court for the trial. The modification removes the licensee's right to have disputes resolved in a venue that aligns with their residency, potentially causing undue hardship and undermining their access to justice, which constitutes a significant departure from established legal standards regarding venue selection.
